prompt: Create a feature spec for adding chat integration to the Dashboard page. The Alerts page already has chat — follow the same pattern. The AI should be able to help users navigate between panels, change time ranges, toggle fullscreen on a panel, switch to edit mode, change template variables, and export the dashboard JSON. Tools that mutate state (edit mode, save) need confirmation. The dashboard has more complex state than alerts — panels with metric queries, template variables, a Zustand view store, and URL-driven tabs. The context should include enough dashboard state for the LLM to answer questions like 'which panel has the highest CPU usage?' without extra API calls. Don't bother with a probing phase, just generate the spec directly.

assertions.description: Spec has all required sections including Acceptance Criteria
No code fences (```) anywhere in the spec outside Implementation Notes
assertions.check: Count code fences (```) in the spec. They should be zero outside any 'Implementation Notes' section. If Implementation Notes exists, code fences there are OK.

assertions.description: No TypeScript interface/type definitions in Design Decisions
assertions.check: The Design Decisions section must not contain 'interface ', 'type ', ': string', ': number', ': boolean', '=> Promise', or other TypeScript type syntax

assertions.description: No JSON object literals or proto field listings in Design Decisions
assertions.check: The Design Decisions section must not contain JSON-like shapes ({ key: value }), proto field enumerations, or data structure definitions. Describing what data is needed in prose is OK; defining the shape is not.

assertions.description: Scope items describe capabilities/behaviors, not exact file paths
assertions.check: The In Scope section must not contain paths like 'src/features/...', 'NEW: src/...', or file extensions like '.ts', '.tsx'. Scope items should describe WHAT the feature does, not WHERE files go.

assertions.description: Spec is under 120 lines (excluding Implementation Notes appendix)
Design Decisions paragraphs open with bold topic sentences that state decisions as facts
assertions.check: Read the Design Decisions section. Each substantive paragraph should open with a **bold sentence** that states a design decision as a fact. An implementer reading ONLY the bold sentences should be able to reconstruct the architectural plan. Score: do >80% of paragraphs follow this pattern?

assertions.description: An implementer could start coding from this spec without asking questions
assertions.check: Read the entire spec. Could a competent React/TypeScript developer who knows this codebase start implementing from this spec without asking clarifying questions about the approach? The spec should name which patterns to follow, which state management approach to use, what each tool does, and how state coordination works. Mark FAIL if any core tool behavior or state coordination decision is missing or ambiguous.

assertions.description: Design Decisions section contains decisions, not implementation
assertions.check: Read the Design Decisions section (NOT Implementation Notes if present). Flag as FAIL if it contains ANY of: (1) code snippets or code fences, (2) TypeScript interface/type definitions, (3) JSON shapes or object literals, (4) exact line numbers, (5) tables mapping features to implementation mechanisms/handlers, (6) useEffect/useState/useMemo implementation details, (7) exact new file paths. Naming modules ('the view store', 'the context hook') is OK. Defining their contents ('interface DashboardToolsOptions { ... }') is NOT OK.

assertions.description: Failure modes state policy decisions, not just error types
assertions.check: Read the Failure Modes section. Each entry should describe a scenario AND a recovery policy that the team could reasonably disagree on. 'Tool called before dashboard loads → return error' is too obvious. 'Panel query fails mid-conversation → degrade to showing last cached data vs showing an error state' IS policy. Are >50% of failure modes genuine policy decisions?

assertions.description: Scope items describe capabilities and behaviors, not files or architecture
assertions.check: Read the In Scope section. Each item should describe a user-facing capability or behavior ('Navigate between dashboard panels via chat', 'Change time ranges'). Items that name specific files, hooks, or components ('useSnapshotChatContext hook', 'NEW: src/features/chat/tools/useDashboardTools.ts') are over-specified. Are >80% of scope items behavioral?

assertions.description: Spec has a well-formed Acceptance Criteria section with behavioral 'done' conditions
assertions.check: Find the Acceptance Criteria section. It must exist as a ## heading. Check: (1) Contains 4-8 checkbox items (- [ ]). (2) Each item is a behavioral/observable outcome ('Chat sidebar appears on the Dashboard page'), NOT an implementation task ('Create useDashboardTools hook') or vague quality ('Chat works well'). (3) Items are collectively sufficient — if all pass, is the feature shippable? FAIL if: section is missing, has fewer than 4 items, items are implementation tasks rather than behavioral outcomes, or items don't cover the core feature behaviors described in the prompt (panel navigation, time ranges, fullscreen, edit mode, template variables, export, confirmation for mutations).

assertions.description: Verification section includes agent-actionable checks with action → outcome format
assertions.check: Read the Verification section. Check: (1) There is an 'Agent-Verifiable' subsection (or equivalent that clearly indicates agent-executable checks). (2) Agent-verifiable checks use an action → expected outcome format — they describe what to DO and what to OBSERVE, not just what should be true. (3) The checks are concrete enough that an agent with browser automation tools could execute them. FAIL if: all non-build checks are labeled 'Manual' or 'Human-Only', OR agent-verifiable checks are vague ('verify chat works') rather than specific ('Open the dashboard page → chat sidebar toggle appears').
